coredata.dc:title: Synergetic effect of carbon dots as co-catalyst for enhanced photocatalytic performance of methyl orange on ZnIn2S4 microspheres
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Lv, Huachang
Yuan, Songliu
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: To design highly-effective photocatalysts including various fascinating properties of eco-friendly, visible-light response and good durability has been strongly desired in photocatalysis field. Here, we synthetised a series of different proportions of carbon dots (CDs) as an ideal cocatalyst to enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4 microspheres in the range of visible light. Simultaneously, CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 hybrid photocatalyst exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for degradating methyl orange (MO) (10mg/L; 100% degradation within 40min), which is 2.34 times higher than that of ZnIn2S4 alone. Additionally, the crystalline phases, structure, specific surface area and optical properties of as-prepared samples were characterized. The mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/ZnIn2S4 composite was also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
ZnIn2S4
Noble-metal-free
Co-catalyst
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Znln2S4 and CDs/Znln2S4 composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology , the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) ( 2012CB825803 , 2013CB932702 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51422207 , 51132006 , 51572179 , 21471106 , 21501126 ), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education ( 20123201110018 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China ( BK20140310 , BK20141305 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2014M560445 , 2015T80581 ), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite; (b) SEM image of a single CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 particle for EDS; (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite; (e–h) EDS element mapping data of C, S, ln and Zn elements throughout a single CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 microsphere.

(a) XRD patterns of pristine ZnIn2S4 and CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite; (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite; (c) UV–vis diffusive reflectance spectra of pristine Znln2S4 and CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite. (Inset: digital photograph of catalyst grains); (d) the full survey spectrum of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite.
XPS spectra of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 sample: (a) C 1s, (b) Zn 2p, (c) In 3d, and (d) S 2p of CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 composite.

(a) Photocatalytic activities of the pure Znln2S4 and CDs/Znln2S4 with different contents of CDs for MO degradation under visible light. (b) Changes of the characteristic absorption of MO by using CDs(5)/Znln2S4 as a photocatalyst. (Inset: the color changes of the MO solutions in line with the degradation time). (c) Kinetic constants for photocatalytic degradation of MO with as-prepared products under visible light irradiation. (d) The repeated bleaching of MO over CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite recycled photocatalyst under visible light.

(a) Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms and pore size, (b) photoluminescence spectra, (c) transient photocurrent responses and (d) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of pure Znln2S4 and CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite.

(a) Trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC over CDs(5)/Znln2S4 sample under visible light irradiation; (b) The ESR spectra of CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 composite under dark and visible light (λ>420nm): DMPO-
O2 − in methanol dispersions and DMPO-
OH in aqueous dispersions.
The possible mechanism of photocatalytic reaction process for degradation of MO over CDs/ZnIn2S 4 composite.

head.ce:title: Synergetic effect of carbon dots as co-catalyst for enhanced photocatalytic performance of methyl orange on ZnIn2S4 microspheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu United Chemical Co., Ltd., East of Yangzhong Bridge, 212212 Yangzhong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To design highly-effective photocatalysts including various fascinating properties of eco-friendly, visible-light response and good durability has been strongly desired in photocatalysis field. Here, we synthetised a series of different proportions of carbon dots (CDs) as an ideal cocatalyst to enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4 microspheres in the range of visible light. Simultaneously, CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 hybrid photocatalyst exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for degradating methyl orange (MO) (10mg/L; 100% degradation within 40min), which is 2.34 times higher than that of ZnIn2S4 alone. Additionally, the crystalline phases, structure, specific surface area and optical properties of as-prepared samples were characterized. The mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/ZnIn2S4 composite was also discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Heterogeneous photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) is thought to be a “green” technology for removing various kinds of contaminants (especially residual soluble dyes in water) utilizing solar energy [1,2]. Semiconductors are usually served as photocatalysts because they can absorb light over a wide spectral range [3,4]. Nevertheless, wide band gap semiconductor based photocatalysts (such as TiO2 [5], ZnO [6], and SnO2 [7]) can be solely activated by ultraviolet (UV) light, which greatly restricts their practical applications. Consequently, exploring effective visible-light-driven photocatalysts has been strongly required in photocatalysis area. Among the traditional semiconductor photocatalysts, many binary metal sulfides with narrow band gaps have been appreciably noticed for photocatalytic degradation organic dyes under visible light irradiation. However, as reported previously, binary metal sulfides are unstable during the photocatalytic reaction because of photocorrosion, thus leading to environmental secondary pollution [8–10]. Fortunately, in 2003, Lei et al. synthetised ZnIn2S4 catalyst using a hydrothermal method, and showed high and stable photocatalytic activity in the field of photocatalysis [11]. Since then, a growing number of researchers have been focusing on the ternary metal chalcogenide ZnIn2S4 due to its more considerable chemical stability and eco-friendly property. Meanwhile, several research groups have also verified that a variety of dyes can be degraded over ZnIn2S4, which further indicates ZnIn2S4 is a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for environmental rehabilitation [12–15]. Regrettably, high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and low migration ability of the photoexcited charge carriers are still negative effects on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of ZnIn2S4 [16,17]. In order to enhance the photocatalytic performance of ZnIn2S4, enormous efforts have been attempted such as controlling different morphologies [18], doping with metals [19] and designing ZnIn2S4-based composite photocatalysts [20]. Among the above methods, the modification of semiconductors using noble metals (Pt, Au and Ag) as co-catalysts has gained increasing research attention especially in heterogeneous photocatalysis [21–23]. Nevertheless, in the view of economizing resources, the rareness and high cost make its manufacture on a large scale obstructed. Hence, there have been growing interests in exploiting alternative noble-metal-free co-catalysts with low cost and earth abundant.

Recently, extensive studies have been focused on carbon dots (CDs) with fascinating properties such as innocuity, chemical stability, mass production and low budget [24,25]. In addition, the photoinduced electron transfer property of CDs can effectively hinder recombination of electron/hole pairs in CDs-based hybrid photocatalysts to improve their photocatalytic activities [26,27]. Furthermore, in our previous reports, CDs/Cu2O and CDs/Ag3PO4 complex systems were demonstrated that both composites showed excellent photocatalytic properties not only in the visible light but also in the (N)IR due to the introduction of CDs, indicating CDs also play an important role in enhancing the optical absorption in the range of board spectrum [28,29]. Considering such preeminent properties of CDs and the limitations of ZnIn2S4, we heuristically attempted to combine CDs with ZnIn2S4 for the improvement of photocatalytic ability of ZnIn2S4 under visible light irradiation.

Herein, CDs/ZnIn2S4 complex photocatalysts were synthetised by a one-step hydrothermal method. Meanwhile, compared with pristine ZnIn2S4, a dramatic enhancement in the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under visible light irradiation is observed over the CDs/ZnIn2S4 microspheres. Meanwhile, the key role of CDs in complex photocatalysts was thoroughly investigated. Furthermore, the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/ZnIn2S4 is also discussed in detail.

Zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), Indium(III) nitrate hydrate In(NO3)3·6H2O and L-cysteine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China, and graphite rods, were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purifications.

We prepared CDs through the typical electrochemical method. Generally, two graphite rods were placed into ultrapure water, and a bias of 30V was applied between two electrodes using a direct current power supply. After a continuous stirring for 120h, a dark yellow solution was formed in the reaction beaker. After filtration of large size CDs, the dark yellow solution was dried. The powder collected was weighted and added to ultrapure water to form the CDs solution of 500mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Znln2S4 and CDs/Znln2S4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 0.2125mmol of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 0.425mmol of In(NO3)3·6H2O, and 1.7mmol of l-cysteine were added into 20ml of ionized water. Then, this mixture was ultrasonically treated for 30min before transferring into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 25mL. After that, the autoclave was heated at 180°C for 12h in an electric oven. When the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, the yellow-green suspension of Znln2S4 was collected and washed with ethanol and deionized water for several times, and finally dried at 60°C for a night. For preparation of CDs/Znln2S4, the CDs solution (500mg/L) with different milliliter number was added to the fabricated process of Znln2S4. The obtained samples were named CDs (X) (X=1, 3, 5, 7)/Znln2S4 samples, which X stands for different milliliter number of CDs solution.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα irradiation. The morphology and structure of the sample were tested by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI-Tecnai F20). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were characterized by NEXUS870 (NICOLET, USA). X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to identify chemical states of the catalysts on an ESCALABMK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg Kα X-ray (hυ =1283.3eV). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed using a Lambda 750 (Perkin-Elmer) spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) study was recorded on at room temperature using a Fluoromax-4 Spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas and pore structures were characterized by a Micromeritics ASAP-2050 porosimeter. For photocurrent time (PT) analysis, the 300W Xe lamp (cutting off λ <420nm) and Na2SO4 (0.5M) was employed as light source and electrolyte. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded by using an alternating voltage of 5mV amplitude in the frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz with the open circuit voltage in 0.5M Na2SO4. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of spin-trapped oxidative radicals were examined on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer.

Briefly, 50mg photocatalysts were put into in 100mL (10mg/L) aqueous solution dye solutions. Prior to irradiation, the suspensions were stirred for 30min in the dark to ensure the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Visible light (300W, xenon lamp) was obtained by using cut-off filters to remove light of λ <420nm. At given time intervals, 3mL aliquots were extracted and centrifugated in the reaction process. And concentrations of dyes were analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy.

The preparation of CDs could be obtained by a typical electrochemical method. The TEM image of the CDs is shown in Fig. S1a, which is similar to the CDs structure reported in previous work [30]. And the size distribution of CDs is displayed in Fig. S1b, which implies that their diameters are in the range of 2–8nm. Here, the CDs(5)/Znln2S4 microspheres was prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method and the morphology of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite is presented in Fig.1 a–d. Particularly, Fig.1a shows that marigold-like CDs(5)/Znln2S4 microspheres are composed of a large number of nanosheets, and the microspheres tend to adhere to each other. Notably, the morphology of individual CDs/ZnIn2S4 (Fig.1b) is nearly the same as that of pure ZnIn2S4 (Fig. S2), indicating the CDs loaded does not affect the growth of ZnIn2S4. However, from the SEM images of CDs(5)/Znln2S4, it is difficult to observe the present of CDs. Therefore, the images of TEM and HRTEM of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 are performed to further demonstrate the microstructure of the CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4. In Fig.1c, some uneven nanosheets with the thickness of 8–10nm formed fringes of the microsphere can be seen obviously. HRTEM image of the local structure region of CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 (Fig.1c) is illustrated in Fig.1d, which clearly exhibits that the complex photocatalyst consists of ZnIn2S4 and CDs, and CDs is distributed intimately on the nanosheet surface of ZnIn2S4. It can be speculated that the intimate interfaces between the ZnIn2S4 and CDs may be a key factor to facilitate the photogenerated charge carrier transfer from ZnIn2S4 to CDs, thus enhancing photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4 photocatalyst. And the lattice fringes of 0.325 and 0.32nm can be attributed to the (102) and (002) plane of ZnIn2S4 and CDs, respectively [17,31]. Additionally, the formation of the CDs/ZnIn2S4 composite was further verified by the EDS elemental mapping of a single CDs(5)/Znln2S4 microsphere (Fig.1e–h) from Fig.1b. Maps of C-K, S-K, ln-L and Zn-K have the same shape and location, confirming the existence of CDs and Znln2S4 in the CDs/ZnIn2S4 hybrid photocatalyst.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of as-synthesized CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4 samples are observed in Fig.2 a. And the diffraction peaks can be perfectly indexed as a hexagonal phase of ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS No. 65-2023) [32,33]. For the CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite, the main characteristic diffraction peaks are similar to those of pristine ZnIn2S4. With respect to the invisibility of the characteristic peak for carbon in the composite, this may be attributed to the small quantity of CDs and relatively low diffraction intensity [28]. The FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs(5)/Znln2S4 complex photocatalyst are shown in Fig.2b. For CDs (blue 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 2, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 line), several peaks at approximately 3447cm−1, 1631cm−1, 1403cm−1 and 1243cm−1 are ascribed to the OH, CO, CC and COH, respectively [34], demonstrating CDs with some functional groups and the oxygen-containing groups can be provided with hydrophilicity. In the case of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 (red line), the peaks for CDs are also observed in the composite. Nevertheless, some dominant peaks in the CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite show slight shifts, for example, the peak of CC shifts about 5cm−1 is mainly resulted from the strong electrostatic interactions between Znln2S4 and CDs [35]. The optical properties of the as-synthesized samples were investigated through UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and given in Fig.2c. It can be clearly seen that the absorption edge of pure Znln2S4 appears at ∼476nm, corresponding to the band gap of around 2.6eV, which is consistent with the previous report [36]. Compared with pure ZnIn2S4, CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite displays markedly red shifts, and the color of the ZnIn2S4 powders changes from faint yellow to bottle green (the inset of Fig.2c), which favors the absorption intensity in the region of visible light, implying that CDs(5)/Znln2S4 composite may produce more photo-generated charges than pure Znln2S4 under visible light illumination and CDs have a crucial influence on utilizing sunlight. Meanwhile, the surface chemical composition of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 photocatalyst was corroborated by XPS. The survey spectrum of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 indicates that C 1s, Zn 2p, In 3d and S 2p exist in the CDs/Znln2S4 complex photocatalyst (Fig.2d). And the high-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s, Zn 2p, In 3d and S 2p regions for CDs(5)Znln2S4 composite are analyzed in the following section.

Fig.3 a displays C 1s spectrum with three peaks at 284.6, 285.4 and 288.2eV matching well with CC, CO/COC and OC bonding, respectively [37]. As can be seen in Fig.3b, the high resolution XPS spectrum of Zn 2p shows two clear peaks about 1022.1eV and 1045.2eV, characterized of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, which is in line with those of Znln2S4 [17]. As for In 3d XPS spectrum (Fig.3c), the peaks at 444.6eV and 452.2eV are assigned to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively [33]. And S 2p (160.3eV for 2p3/2 and 162.0eV for 2p1/2) also can be observed for the prepared sample, corresponding to the S2− in ZnIn2S4 (Fig.3d) [34]. These above results further give solid evidences of the formation of CDs/Znln2S4 composite.

The photocatalytic activities of pure Znln2S4 and relevant CDs/Znln2S4 composites were tested through degradation of MO in aqueous solution. As can be seen in Fig.4 a, the blank test demonstrates self-photolysis of MO is limited without photocatalysts, indicating that MO is stable and difficult to be photolysis. Although visible active co-catalyst-free ZnIn2S4 gives the degradation ratio of 98% after 120min under visible light irradiation, dramatic enhancements in the degradation of MO were obtained with the introduction of CDs on the surface of ZnIn2S4 microspheres. Notably, when increasing the amount of as-prepared CDs solution from 1 to 5mL, the degradation time decreases gradually from 120min to 40min for totally removal MO. Further increasing the content of CDs over 5mL leads an obvious decrease of degradation efficiency. The phenomenon may be due to that the enhanced photocatalytic activity with the increasing amounts of CDs less than 5mL can attribute the increasing density of active sites on the surface of Znln2S4, and the decreased photocatalytic activity loading the CDs amount over 5mL results from the shading effect [38]. In short, CDs(5)/Znln2S4 hybrid photocatalyst exhibits the highest photocatalytic performance for completely degrading MO in 40min. Additionally, Fig.4b shows the changes of characteristic absorption of MO (at 464nm) in 40min under the visible-light irradiation over CDs(5)/Znln2S4, and the inset of Fig.4b discloses the color changes of MO solutions corresponding to the degradation time. Fig. S3 illustrates the kinetics of MO photodegradation in the absence and presence of photocatalysts, and the kinetics equation can be displayed as follows [39]:
(1)
ln
C
0
/ C = k t
where C0 represents the initial MO concentration, C is the MO concentration at the reaction time t (min), k is the first order rate constant (min−1). The kinetics constants of MO degradation with different products are calculated by making a linear plot of ln(C0/C) against time. The results are illustrated in Fig.4c. It is noted that the k value of pure Znln2S4 is 0.0401min−1. The k constants of CDs/Znln2S4 composites are larger than that of pure Znln2S4. Especially, the CDs(5)/Znln2S4 hybrid material has a largest value of 0.09387min−1, which is 2.34 times as large as pure Znln2S4. In addition, the stability of catalysts is an important factor for the highly efficient photocatalyst. Furthermore, the catalytic stability of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 is evaluated by reusing it toward the photocatalytic decomposition of MO for five cycles. Remarkably, its photocatalytic activity did not decrease significantly after five consecutive runs (Fig.4d), indicating that the CDs(5)/Znln2S4 can work as efficient photocatalyst for organic pollutants degradation. Furthermore, we have investigated the XRD of sample after five cycles and the XRD analysis (Fig. S4) also proved that the crystal structure of the CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 composite did not change. Additionally, other dyes such as rhodamine B (Rh B, 10mg/L) and methyl blue (MB, 10mg/L) were further studied over as-prepared samples for confirming the excellent photocatalytic activity. From Fig. S5, it is observed that the photodegradation rate of Rh B over CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 reached 63% after 40min visible light irradiation, and the photodegradation rate of 92% for the degradation of MB is observed.

To reveal the origin of high photocatalytic activities of CDs/Znln2S4 composites, multiple techniques were applied to characterize as-prepared samples. Fig.5 a shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of pure Znln2S4 and CDs(5)/Znln2S4. The S BET of CDs(5)/Znln2S4 (110.56cm3/g−1) is a little higher than that of pure ZnIn2S4 (104.15cm3/g−1), the pore sizes of which are 4.5nm and 5.2nm, respectively. From the above datas, we can find that the introduction of CDs did not obviously alter the specific surface area of pure ZnIn2S4, but the specific surface areas of slight change do affect the photocatalytic performance. It is reported larger specific area of hybrid photocatalyst favors the enhancement of its photocatalytic activity due to more surface active sites for the adsorption of reactant molecules [34]. As is known, photocatalytic activity is in relation to the trapping and lifetime of photogenerated electrons and holes in the photocatalyst. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra can provide the effectiveness of trapping, migration and transfer of charge carriers because the PL emission comes from the recombination of free charge carriers. The fluorescence intensity is directly proportional to the electron-hole recombination probability, and lower PL intensity indicates the decrease in the recombination rate [40]. Fig.5b displays the PL spectra of pure ZnIn2S4 and CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 samples. As is shown, CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 shares lower fluorescence intensity than ZnIn2S4, indicating that efficient interfacial electron transfer from ZnIn2S4 to CDs in the CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 system, which may be favored to suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Fig.5c shows the photocurrent-time (PT) testing curves of pure ZnIn2S4 and CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 samples. It can be seen that CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 hybrid (0.13μAcm−2) material has a higher photocurrent response than pure ZnIn2S4 (0.08μAcm−2), indicating that CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 hybrid material has a higher separation rate of photoexcited electrons and holes than ZnIn2S4 alone under visible light irradiation, and further illustrates that CDs is an effective active co-catalyst to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers in ZnIn2S4. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra are also used to investigate the charge transfer resistance and separation efficiency between the photo-generated electrons and holes [41]. In general, a smaller arc size in an EIS Nyquist plot means a smaller charge-transfer resistance on the electrode surface [42]. As shown in Fig.5d, the electrode of CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 displays much smaller arc size than that of pure ZnIn2S4, suggesting it is an effective separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs for the CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 composite. Therefore, these consistent PL, PT and EIS measurements all proved that the introduction of CDs can effectively improve the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs of ZnIn2S4, thus enhancing photocatalytic ability for the degradation of MO.

In order to further ascertain the photocatalytic mechanism of CDs/Znln2S4 composite, we used trapping experiments and ESR spin-trap technique with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolineNoxide (DMPO) to determine main active species in the photocatalytic process (Fig.6 a and b). In Fig.6a, the degradation rate of MO is only slightly decreased by 19% when adding EDTA (h + quencher), suggesting that h + is not the main active specie for the degradation of MO. However, the removal yields of MO are relatively decreased in the presence of IPA, which suggests
OH has a definite effect on the degradation of MO. It is interesting to note that, when BQ as a scavenger for
O2 − is added, the degradation behavior of MO is significantly further decreased. The above results suggest that
O2 − is the main active specie and
OH and h + are the secondary active specie for the degradation of MO. The ESR spin-trap technique with DMPO was employed to further confirm reactive oxygen species generated on the surface of photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. Fig.6b reveals ESR spectra measured as the effect of with light or without irradiation over the CDs(5)/ZnIn2S4 photocatalyst at room temperature. The four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH species and six characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − species are observed under visible light irradiation and no such signals of
OH and
O2 − can be detected in the dark, implying that
O2 − and
OH radicals exist in the CDs/ZnIn2S4(5) samples under visible light irradiation, which are active oxidation species leading to the photodegradation of MO.

According to the above analysis and experiment results, we provide a tentative photocatalytic mechanism of CDs/Znln2S4 hybrid photocatalysts (Fig. 7 ). Firstly, Znln2S4 absorbs visible light and transfers electrons from its valence band to conduction band (CB), leaving the h + in the valence band (VB) of Znln2S4. Usually, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be quickly recombined without co-catalysts loaded, resulting in the poor photocatalystic activity of pure ZnIn2S4. Moreover, CDs have a lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level than Znln2S4, when CDs were anchored on the surface of ZnIn2S4, the photogenerated electrons from the CB of Znln2S4 can easily transfer to CDs through photo-induced charge transfer process and the h+ leaves in the valence band of ZnIn2S4 for CDs can act as both electron acceptors and donors [43], thus effectively improves charge separation by the rapid photo-induced charge transfer. Subsequently, the photo-electrons on the CDs could further combine with oxygen in the aqueous solution to form
O2 −, which is a strong oxidizing agent to decompose MO. Meanwhile, the VB position of Znln2S4 (1.60eV vs. NHE) is more negative than the standard redox potential of E (
OH/OH−) (1.99eV vs. NHE) [44], resulting in the holes of ZnIn2S4 cannot oxidize immediately OH− or H2O to
OH radicals and the
OH radicals mainly originates from the reaction of
O2 − with photogenerated electrons and H2O and partial h+ in the VB of ZnIn2S4 can directly oxidize the pollutants.

In summary, noble-metal-free and eco-friendly CDs co-catalyst modified Znln2S4 hybrid materials have been successfully prepared using a facile hydrothermal process. CDs as a cocatalyst in the photocatalyst can efficiently transfer photo-generated electrons from CB of Znln2S4 to CDs, hindering the recombination of electron-hole pairs of Znln2S4, which makes CDs/Znln2S4 exhibit excellent photocatalytic performances than that of pristine ZnIn2S4. In addition, the CDs/Znln2S4 hybrid photocatalyst exhibits excellent stability and reusability, which may be meaningful for the practical applications in environmental water purification. This work provides an economic and efficient method to design and fabricate highly-effective hybrid photocatalysts for the photocatalytic oxidation reaction by using non-precious carbon nanomaterials as cocatalyst to decorate other semiconductor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (2012CB825803, 2013CB932702), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51132006, 51572179, 21471106, 21501126), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123201110018), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20140310, BK20141305), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014M560445, 2015T80581), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2016.11.013.
